title: Carbon Brief’s ranking of the most highly cited institutions
gist: Carbon Brief's Cosmos 500 institution ranking shows climate-research capacity heavily concentrated in the US (183 of 500 institutions, 13 of top 20), with France's CNRS top overall, China and the global south barely represented — set against deep Trump-era cuts to US climate science.

insights.claim: 183 of the 500 institutions in Carbon Brief's Cosmos 500 ranking — and 13 of the top 20 — are based in the US, with the UK next at 53, then Australia (33), Germany (31), France (30) and Canada (29).
insights.speaker: Ayesha Tandon, Kerry Cleaver, Joe Goodman, Tom Pearson, Tom Prater
insights.so_what: Quantifies extreme US concentration of climate-research capacity — making US science funding policy a single point of systemic risk.
insights.quote: 183 institutions in the Cosmos 500 ranking – as well as 13 of the top 20 – are based in the US.

insights.claim: Only 30 institutions from global south countries appear in the Cosmos 500, around half of which are in China.
insights.speaker: Ayesha Tandon, Kerry Cleaver, Joe Goodman, Tom Pearson, Tom Prater
insights.so_what: Documents the structural underrepresentation of global-south climate research — a persistent equity and capacity gap.
insights.quote: there are only 30 institutions from global south countries in the Cosmos 500, around half of which are in China.

insights.claim: France's CNRS tops the overall ranking with a publication count of 864,154, ahead of the US's NCAR (721,372) and University of Washington (662,032), while China's CAS — the world's largest research organisation per Nature — ranks fourth.
insights.speaker: Ayesha Tandon, Kerry Cleaver, Joe Goodman, Tom Pearson, Tom Prater
insights.so_what: Identifies the institutional pillars of global climate science; CNRS leading despite US institutional volume.

insights.claim: The dominance of US institutions in climate research is being threatened by Trump-administration funding cuts and political interference, with hundreds of NOAA staff fired in spring 2025.
insights.speaker: Ayesha Tandon, Kerry Cleaver, Joe Goodman, Tom Pearson, Tom Prater
insights.so_what: Direct threat to the largest single node of global climate-research capacity — a structural negative for US-led climate science output.
insights.quote: hundreds of staff members at NOAA – which ranks 11th in the Cosmos 500 – were fired.

insights.claim: An estimated 95,000 employees have left US federal science agencies through layoffs, retirements or resignations since Trump returned to the White House.
insights.speaker: Ayesha Tandon, Kerry Cleaver, Joe Goodman, Tom Pearson, Tom Prater
insights.so_what: Scale of the brain drain quantifies a multi-year erosion of US federal scientific capacity.
insights.quote: an estimated 95,000 employees “have left federal science agencies through layoffs, retirements or resignations since Mr Trump returned to the White House last year”.

insights.claim: In April 2025 the Trump administration dismissed all contributors — nearly 400 scientists and experts — to the sixth US national climate assessment, a report used by federal and local governments to prepare for climate impacts.
insights.speaker: Ayesha Tandon, Kerry Cleaver, Joe Goodman, Tom Pearson, Tom Prater
insights.so_what: Removal of the national climate assessment authors degrades the official US evidence base for adaptation planning.
insights.quote: the Trump administration dismissed all contributors to the sixth US national climate assessment over email.

insights.claim: In the IPCC-only ranking, the US's NCAR comes first, CNRS second and the UK Met Office third — a reordering versus the full Cosmos 500.
insights.speaker: Ayesha Tandon, Kerry Cleaver, Joe Goodman, Tom Pearson, Tom Prater
insights.so_what: Shows which institutions most shape IPCC assessments specifically, distinct from the broader literature.

source_meta.author: Ayesha Tandon, Kerry Cleaver, Joe Goodman, Tom Pearson, Tom Prater
